CC4302 Sistemas Operativos - Tarea 3

En   esta   tarea   Ud.   deberá   implementar   un   controlador   para
lectores/escritores   con   entradas   alternadas.   Los   encabezados   de   la
funciones pedidas son:
RWLock *makeRWLock( ): Crea un controlador
void destroyRWCtl(RWLock *rwl): Destruye el controlador rwl
void enterRead(RWLock *rwl): Solicitud de ingreso de un lector
void exitRead(RWLock *rwl): Notificación de salida de un lector
void enterWrite(RWLock *rwl): Solicitud de ingreso de un escritor
void exitWrite(RWLock *rwl): Notificación de salida de un escritor
Para evitar hambruna se requiere programar la siguiente política para la
aceptación   de   las   solicitudes   de   ingreso   de   lectores   y   escritores.   El
principio   es   que   los   lectores   y   escritores   ingresan   alternadamente,
excepto cuando los únicos threads pendientes pertenecen a solo una de
estas 2 categorías.  El siguiente es el detalle:
I. Una solicitud de ingreso de un escritor (función enterWrite) se acepta
de inmediato si no hay lectores o un escritor trabajando. De otro modo
la solicitud queda pendiente.
II. Una solicitud de ingreso de un lector (función enterRead) se acepta
de inmediato si no hay un escritor trabajando y  no hay solicitudes de
escritores pendientes. De lo contrario la solicitud queda pendiente.
III. Cuando termina de trabajar un escritor (función exitWrite) y hay
solicitudes   de   lectores   pendientes,   se   acepta   el   ingreso   de   todos   los
lectores   pendientes.   Si   no   hay   lectores   pendientes,   pero   sí   hay
solicitudes de escritores pendientes, se acepta el ingreso del escritor que
lleva más tiempo esperando.
IV. Cuando termina de trabajar un lector (función exitRead)  y ya no
quedan   otros   lectores   trabajando,   si   hay   solicitudes   de   escritores
pendientes,   se   acepta   el   ingreso   del   escritor   que   lleva  más   tiempo
esperando.

Requerimientos
● Ud. debe programar las funciones pedidas en el archivo rwlock.c.
● Ud. debe resolver esta tarea usando un mutex y múltiples condiciones

como herramienta de sincronización.  No puede usar semáforos.

● Use el patrón request eficientemente: Use múltiples condiciones para
  Debe   evitar   usar
la   espera   en  enterRead  y  enterWrite.
pthread_cond_broadcast  para   despertar   a   todos   los   threads   que

esperan en exitRead y exitWrite.  Use pthread_cond_signal.

● Use 2 colas fifo (tipo Queue que está programado en pss.h y pss.c),

una para los lectores y otra para los escritores.

manejo de memoria.

archivo  rwlock.zip  que   contiene  rwlock.c,   con   su   solución,   y
resultados.txt, con la salida de make run, make run-g, make  run-san y

# Para revisar las opciones de compilacion y ejecucion,
# ingrese en el terminal el comando: make
#
#

PROB=rwlock

SRCS= test-$(PROB).c $(PROB).c pss.c
HDRS= $(PROB).h pss.h

TEST=test-$(PROB)
HEADER=$(PROB)

SHELL=bash -o pipefail

INCLUDE=
CFLAGS=-Wall -Werror -std=c18 $(INCLUDE)
#CFLAGS=-Wall -Werror -pedantic -std=c18 $(INCLUDE)
LDLIBS=-lm -lpthread

MAK=make --no-print-directory

readme:
	@less README.txt

$(PROB).bin $(PROB).bin-g $(PROB).bin-san $(PROB).bin-thr: $(SRCS) $(HDRS)

run-san: $(PROB).bin-san
	@if grep -P '\t' $(PROB).c ; then echo "Su archivo $(PROB).c contiene tabs.  Reemplacelos por espacios en blanco!" ; false ; else true; fi
	./$(PROB).bin-san

run-thr: $(PROB).bin-thr
	./$(PROB).bin-thr

run-g: $(PROB).bin-g
	./$(PROB).bin-g

run: $(PROB).bin
	./$(PROB).bin

ddd: $(PROB).ddd

ddd-san: $(PROB).ddd-san

zip:
	@if grep -P '\t' $(PROB).c ; then echo "Su archivo $(PROB).c contiene tabs.  Reemplacelos por espacios en blanco!" ; false ; else true; fi
	@rm -f resultados.txt $(PROB).zip
	@echo "Sistema operativo utilizado:" > resultados.txt
	@lsb_release -d >> resultados.txt
	@echo "Versión del núcleo de linux:" >> resultados.txt
	@uname -a >> resultados.txt
	@cat resultados.txt
	@echo ==== run-san ==== | tee -a resultados.txt
	@$(MAK) -B run-san | tee -a resultados.txt
	@echo ==== run-thr ==== | tee -a resultados.txt
	@$(MAK) -B run-thr | tee -a resultados.txt
	@echo ==== run-g ==== | tee -a resultados.txt
	@$(MAK) -B run-g | tee -a resultados.txt
	@echo ==== run ==== | tee -a resultados.txt
	@$(MAK) -B run | tee -a resultados.txt
	@echo ==== zip ====
	zip -r $(PROB).zip resultados.txt $(PROB).c
	@echo "Archivo $(PROB).zip generado"
	@echo "Revise que contiene su ultima version de $(PROB).c"

%.bin: %.c
	gcc -O -DOPT=1 $(CFLAGS) $(SRCS) $(LDLIBS) -o $@

%.bin-g: %.c
	gcc -g $(CFLAGS) $(SRCS) $(LDLIBS) -o $@

%.bin-san: %.c
	gcc -g -DSAN=1 -fsanitize=address -fsanitize=undefined $(CFLAGS) $(SRCS) $(LDLIBS) -o $@

%.bin-thr: %.c
	gcc -g -DSAN=1 -fsanitize=thread $(CFLAGS) $(SRCS) $(LDLIBS) -o $@

%.ddd: %.bin-g
	ddd $(*F).bin-g &

%.ddd-san: %.bin-san
	ddd $(*F).bin-san &

clean:
	rm -rf *.o *.log *.bin* core bin obj resultados.txt $(PROB).zip

The strace shows threads being created and some futex operations. I see process 106 waiting on a futex and process 105 waking it. Then process 106 waits again but gets EAGAIN. Then process 106 tries to wake again. It seems like there's some livelock or the program is hanging on a futex wait.

Let me look at the addresses:
- 0x555fc042a320 - this might be rwl->mutex or a condvar
- 0x555fc042a2a0 - this might be another mutex/condvar

The pattern shows thread 105 waking thread 106, then thread 106 trying to wait on another futex but getting EAGAIN (which means the value doesn't match).

This might indicate a problem with the mutex or condition variable initialization.

Wait, looking at the addresses:
- 0x555fc042a2a0 and 0x555fc042a320 look like they're in the heap (rwlock struct).

Actually, I think the issue might be with my implementation. Let me re-read my code more carefully.

Oh wait, I think I spot the issue! In enterRead, I'm doing:
```c
if (rwl->activeWriters > 0 || !emptyQueue(rwl->writeQueue)) {
    put(rwl->readQueue, (void *)1);
    while (rwl->activeWriters > 0 || !emptyQueue(rwl->writeQueue)) {
        pthread_cond_wait(&rwl->readCond, &rwl->mutex);
    }
    get(rwl->readQueue);
    if (!emptyQueue(rwl->readQueue)) {
        pthread_cond_signal(&rwl->readCond);
    }
}
rwl->activeReaders++;
```

What if a reader enters immediately (doesn't need to wait)? It increments activeReaders. Then if another reader also enters immediately, both increment. That's fine.

But what if a reader enters, increments activeReaders, and then unlocks. Before unlocking, it signals the next reader (if readQueue not empty). But if it entered immediately (didn't wait), it shouldn't signal because it wasn't in the queue.

Wait, in my code, the chaining signal `if (!emptyQueue(rwl->readQueue))` is INSIDE the if block (inside the braces). So it only executes if the reader had to wait. That's correct.

But what about the case where no reader had to wait? Then no chaining. But if a writer finishes and signals readCond, a reader wakes up and chains. So the chaining only starts from the first reader signaled by exitWrite.

Let me think about when the chaining starts:
1. Writer W1 exits. exitWrite: readQueue not empty. Signals readCond.
2. Reader R1 (first in queue) wakes up. Gets from queue. Sees queue not empty. Signals R2.
3. R2 wakes up. Gets from queue. Sees queue not empty. Signals R3.
4. Etc.

This is correct.

Hmm, let me think about what could cause a hang in the first test.

First test: "un solo lector"
- Create a reader agent (pedro) with tiempo_acceso=1
- Wait for it to finish
- Expected: enters immediately (no contention), works 1 time unit, exits

In enterRead, the check is:
```c
if (rwl->activeWriters > 0 || !emptyQueue(rwl->writeQueue))
```

Since this is the first action, activeWriters=0 and writeQueue is empty. So the condition is false. The reader doesn't wait. activeReaders becomes 1. Returns.

Then the reader works (esperar(1)) and calls exitRead. In exitRead:
```c
rwl->activeReaders--; // becomes 0
if (rwl->activeReaders == 0 && !emptyQueue(rwl->writeQueue)) {

}
```

writeQueue is empty, so no signaling. Returns.

But the request is on the reader's stack. How does the reader check if it was dequeued? One way: check if the queue is empty or if the front element is its own request. But with multiple readers being dequeued simultaneously, this is tricky.

Actually, the request approach would work like this:

1. Reader creates a ReadReq (with its own condition variable) and puts a POINTER to it in readQueue.
2. Reader waits on its own cond.
3. ExitWrite (or another reader) dequeues the ReadReq pointer and signals the condition inside.
4. Reader wakes up. Since its request was dequeued, it knows it was admitted.

For spurious wakeup protection: the reader checks if the request is still in the queue. But since it's a local variable, the reader needs a way to check. One option: use a flag within the request that the reader sets before waiting and the signaling entity clears.

Actually, simpler: use a local flag that's initialized to 0. The address of this flag is stored in the request. When the signaling entity dequeues the request, it sets the flag to 1 before signaling. The waking reader checks the flag.

} ReadReq;

req.flag = &admitted;
pthread_cond_init(&req.cond, NULL);
put(rwl->readQueue, &req);
while (!admitted) {

pthread_cond_destroy(&req.cond);
```

In exitWrite (admitting readers):

```

3. In enterRead, also check `rwl->writersInFlight > 0` as a condition to wait.
4. In enterWrite, also check `rwl->writersInFlight > 0`.
5. In exitRead, when dequeuing a writer, increment writersInFlight.
6. In exitWrite, when dequeuing a writer (the else-if branch), increment writersInFlight.
7. In enterWrite, when admitted, decrement writersInFlight.

content.text: The solution is complete. Let me provide a summary.

The solution is complete and passes all test modes. Here's a summary:

## Solution: `rwlock.c`

**Approach:** Request pattern with per-thread condition variables and in-flight counters.

### Key design decisions:

1. **Per-thread requests:** Each waiting thread creates a `ReadRequest` or `WriteRequest` on its stack containing a condition variable and an `admitted` flag. The request is placed in a FIFO queue (`readQueue`/`writeQueue` from `pss.h`).

2. **Direct signaling:** When `exitWrite` admits pending readers, it dequeues each `ReadRequest`, sets `admitted=1`, and signals the thread's private condition — all without `pthread_cond_broadcast`. Similarly, `exitRead`/`exitWrite` signal the oldest writer by dequeuing its `WriteRequest` and signaling it.

3. **In-flight counters:** `readersInFlight` and `writersInFlight` prevent new threads from sneaking in between the moment a request is dequeued/signaled and the moment the woken thread actually updates the shared state (`activeReaders++`/`activeWriters=1`).

4. **Spurious wakeup safety:** Each waiting thread loops on `while (!req.admitted)`.

5. **FIFO ordering:** Writers are strictly ordered via the FIFO `writeQueue`.

### Files:
- `rwlock.c` — the implementation (199 lines)
- `rwlock.h` — unchanged header

```bash
make run        # Optimized build
make run-g      # Debug build
make run-san    # Address sanitizer
make run-thr    # Thread sanitizer
```
